title: 2025 Session 5 exercises and solutions
body:
Session 5: Exercises and solutions


Question 1
The interest rate on one-year risk-free bonds is 5% in the United Kingdom and 3.75% in
Switzerland. The current exchange rate is £0.5 per Swiss franc. Suppose that you are a
British investor and you expect the foreign currency (Swiss franc) to appreciate by 2%
over the next year.
     a. Calculate the foreign currency risk premium, which is defined as the difference
         between the expected exchange rate return E(S) and the interest rate differential.
     b. Based on your result, does the uncovered interest rate parity hold?
     c. Calculate the domestic currency return on the foreign bond, assuming that your
         currency appreciation expectations are met.

Solution:
    a. The interest rate differential (U.K. minus Swiss) = 0.05 − 0.0375 = 0.0125 or
        1.25%. This implies that the Swiss franc trades at a forward premium of 1.25%,
        that is, the forward exchange rate is quoted at a premium of 1.25% over the spot
        exchange rate of £0.5 per franc. But the expected appreciation of the franc is
        higher and equal to 2%. Hence, there is a currency risk premium on the Swiss
        franc:
          Foreign currency risk premium = 0.0200 − 0.0125 = 0.0075 = 0.75%.


    b. No, as the uncovered interest rate parity implies that the expected exchange rate
       return E(S) equals the interest rate differential, i.e. zero risk premium.

    c. The domestic currency (U.K. pounds) return on the foreign bond is 5.75%. This
       can be calculated in one of the following two ways:
        Domestic risk-free rate + Foreign currency risk premium = 5% + 0.75% = 5.75%
        or
        Foreign risk-free rate + Expected exchange rate movement = 3.75% + 2% =
        5.75%.




                                             1
 Question 2
You believe that the U.S. dollar will strongly appreciate against the euro in the next few
weeks, much beyond what is currently priced in the forward rate. What action can you
take?


Solution:
    If you believe that the U.S. dollar will appreciate against the euro over its current
    forward exchange rate, you can take the following action:
    • Today:
    Borrow euros.
    Sell euros and buy U.S. dollars at the current spot rate.
    Lend those U.S. dollars.
    • In a few weeks:
    Sell those U.S. dollars and buy euros at the new spot rate.


    If your assumption turns out to be correct, you will make a profit, unless the interest
    rate differential already reflects the expectation that the dollar will strongly
    appreciate.
    An equivalent action to be taken today is to enter a forward exchange contract to sell
    euros and buy U.S. dollars at the current forward exchange rate, with delivery taking
    place in a few weeks. At maturity, you will make a profit if the spot €/$ has risen
    beyond what is currently priced in the forward rate.
    Other derivative contracts, such as currency options or swaps, can also be used.




                                              2
 Question 3
On June 30, 2024, a company bought a one-year forward contract for receiving USD 1
million for AUD 1.50 million (AUD 1.50 per USD).
   a. Calculate the payoff if the spot exchange rate on June 30, 2025 is AUD 1.55 per
      USD.
      Solution: At the spot market, USD 1 million is traded at AUD 1.55 million, the
      company pay AUD 0.05 million less. Payoff = + AUD 0.05m
   b. Calculate the payoff if the spot exchange rate on June 30, 2025 is AUD 1.45 per
      USD.
      Solution: At the spot market, USD 1 million is traded at AUD 1.45 million, the
      company pay AUD 0.05 million more. Payoff = - AUD 0.05m


Question 4




                                         3
 Assume that we have:
    Spot exchange rate:              AUD 1.50 per USD
    One-year forward rate:           AUD 1.55 per USD
    Risk-free interest rate in USD: 3% (annualized)
    Risk-free interest rate in AUD: 5% (annualized)
   a. A company has to pay a cost of USD 1 million to its supplier next year. To better
      hedge the exchange risk, what can the company do (purchase forward contracts or
      hedge by money market transactions)?
       Solution: think of the present value of the cost. We put money right now in the
       AUD bank (forward contract) or in the USD bank (spot rate)) and use this deposit
       + interest to pay the cost.
       forward contract cost = (USD 1 million * 1.55)/1.05 = AUD 1.4762 million
       money market transaction: (USD 1 million / 1.03)*1.5 = AUD 1.4563 million
       Money market transaction is better.


   b. A company will receive a revenue of USD 1 million from its customer next year.
      To better hedge the exchange risk, what can the company do (purchase forward
      contracts or hedge by money market transactions)?
       Solution: think of the present value of the revenue. We borrow money right now in
       the AUD bank (forward contract) or in the USD bank (spot rate), and pay this
       borrowing using the revenue next year.
       forward contract cost = (USD 1 million * 1.55)/1.05 = AUD 1.4762 million
       money market transaction: (USD 1 million / 1.03)*1.5 = AUD 1.4563 million
       Forward contract is better.



Question 5
Suppose that the forward ask price for March 20 on euros is $0.9127 at the same time that
the price of IMM euro futures for delivery on March 20 is $0.9145. How could an
arbitrageur profit from this situation?




                                             4
 ANSWER. Since the futures price exceeds the forward rate, the arbitrageur should
   sell futures contracts at $0.9145 and buy euro forward in the same amount at
   $0.9127. The arbitrageur will earn (0.9145 - 0.9127=0.0018) per euro arbitraged.




                                        5
